features.title: South-face beds
features.detail: SSE-facing bench near 2056 ft, ~25° — thermal sun and a downhill escape; Penn State collar deer bed exactly this.
features.windNote: Beds hold a NNW wind. Approach from the ridge above. Thermals: at dawn scent drains SSE (downhill); by midday it rises NNW (upslope) — cross-check that against the wind.

features.title: Saddle crossing
features.detail: A ridge saddle near 2001 ft — deer cross ridges at their low gaps, funneling travel between bedding areas. Prime stand.
features.windNote: Sit the downhill side on a crosswind. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.

features.windNote: Deer walk it into the wind; sit the downwind end, above or below the trail. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.

features.windNote: Deer move it toward feed in the evening, back to bed at dawn — hunt it downwind of the trail. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.

features.title: Road crossing
features.detail: A bed-to-feed route meets a road 0 m out. Deer cross an opening at the same habitual spots rather than wherever they happen to reach it, so a crossing with cover tight to both sides concentrates travel into a few yards. Hunt the cover, back off the road itself.
features.windNote: Sit off the road on a crosswind so your scent crosses the opening rather than running back down the trail. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.

features.title: Water crossing
features.detail: Where a bed-to-feed route crosses a drainage — deer funnel through the low gap to get across, with reliable water right there. One of the highest-odds stands on the property.
features.windNote: Sit the downwind bank. Morning thermals sink into the drainage, so hunt the low end at first light. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.

features.title: Terrain funnel
features.detail: A ~60 m neck of travelable ground squeezed between steep faces, open the other way — deer pour through it. A high-odds stand: sit the downwind edge of the neck.
features.windNote: Cover the neck on a crosswind so your scent blows off the travel lane, not down it. Thermals: scent sinks downhill at dawn/dusk, rises upslope by midday — let the active thermal, not just the wind, pick your side.
